slides.heading: Before Git: every project looked like this
slides.body:
You finished working on something, so you save a copy just in case.

Two weeks later, you have 6 versions of the same file. Which one is the real one? What changed between them? Who knows?

This is the problem Git was built to solve.
bullets: 🗑️ Afraid to delete old files in case you need them
🤝 Emailing files back and forth with teammates
🐛 No way to know when a bug was introduced

slides.heading: Git gives your project a real timeline
slides.body:
Instead of a pile of copies, Git gives you a clean, labelled history.

Every change is recorded with a message explaining what you did and why. You can scroll back through the entire life of your project — like a time machine.

Click the commits in the visualizer to explore what each one changed.
bullets: 📅 Every change is timestamped and labelled
🔎 Find who introduced a bug and when
↩️ Undo anything, even from a week ago
📖 Understand the whole story of your project

slides.heading: Branches: experiment without fear
slides.body:
Imagine you want to try a risky new feature, but you don't want to break what's already working.

With Git, you can create a parallel timeline called a branch. You work on your experiment there. If it works, you merge it in. If it doesn't, you just delete the branch — nothing in your main project was touched.

This is one of the most powerful ideas in software development.
bullets: 🗑️ Delete if it doesn't — no damage done
👥 Teams work in parallel branches simultaneously

slides.heading: The staging area — your packing zone
slides.body:
When you're ready to make a snapshot, Git doesn't automatically save everything.

Instead, you choose exactly what to include — like packing a suitcase. You pick which files go in, then take the snapshot.

This is called staging, and it lets you make clean, focused commits instead of messy all-at-once saves.

Click the files to move them through the stages!
bullets: git add moves files to staging → git commit saves them permanently
